message.content:
Try HARD to REFUTE this proposed lever for improving a RACE score. Default to refuted=true when uncertain.

LEVER: Padding cut to fund the synthesis budget
TARGETS: instruction_following (0.25) / Consistent Focus on Restructuring Impact w=0.20; readability P1 'appropriate information density and avoidance of redundancy' w=0.15 (weight 0.25)
CHANGE: Deletion pass over the composed body: (1) metadata-as-content sentences ('published in the Strategic Management Journal', 'received 239 Scopus citations', ALMATO bot counts [36], '525 papers... only 20 papers' [103]); (2) off-topic digressions (AI-lit-review rejection [91][92], Toptal ad content [55], call-center market sizing [99], resume/hiring-process surveys [54] unless reframed as labor-market matching); (3) same-fact-two-sources duplicates (the ChatGPT '2x as likely to report using it' sentence appears verbatim twice, lines 19 and 27, cited to [41] and [61] — catch via fuzzy-match dedup across DIFFERENT citation keys, which is why every existing dedup missed it). Spend the freed ~1,500-2,000 words on the synthesis paragraphs and sector section — this is what keeps total length at reference parity.
CLAIMED GAIN: +0.005 to +0.010 direct; primarily an ENABLER — without it, levers 3-4 either blow past 9k words or cannibalize coverage
CLAIMED FAILURE MODE: Over-aggressive deletion cuts facts the judge counts for breadth, re-opening the comprehensiveness gap; fuzzy dedup at low thresholds merges genuinely distinct findings (e.g. the two different 5.4%-adoption stats in [94] and [95] are the same fact, but the 15% [42] vs 13.8% [71] productivity numbers are different specifications of the same study — keep one, say why). Human-review the deletion list once before automating.

ATTACK IT THROUGH THIS LENS:
MEASUREMENT: would this lever actually move the RACE judge, or only an internal proxy? Recall RACE is reference-based: Overall = target/(target+reference). A change that improves our report but that the JUDGE cannot see, or that the reference ALSO does well, yields ~0 points.

Context: our report /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md scored 0.4313. Reference /home/polaris/polaris_project/RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md = 0.5000 by definition. SOTA = 0.5265.
Read the files if you need to check a claim. Be adversarial, concrete, and brief.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md:19:The study also documents a 1.2 percentage point decrease in the probability of receiving any employment in a given month—approximately a 10% drop relative to baseline—and a 4.7% reduction in the number of jobs conditional on being employed.[38] By contrast, aggregate labor-market indicators through 2024–2025 show limited economywide disruption; one analysis reports that 35.9% of U.S. workers used generative AI by December 2025 but finds only small positive wage effects and no statistically significant declines in job openings or employment in exposed occupations.[39] A prominent Harvard University study tracking 62 million workers across 285,000 U.S. firms finds that junior positions have been shrinking at companies integrating AI since 2023, with researchers warning that AI is eroding the bottom rungs of career ladders.[40] This finding is consistent with online labor market evidence that entry-level and less-experienced workers in highly exposed occupations bear the brunt of early displacement effects.[38][39] In quantitative terms, treated participants completed the post-treatment task 11 minutes faster—a 0.75 standard deviation reduction—while evaluator grades increased by 0.45 standard deviations.[41] The experiment also documents a reduction in productivity inequality: the correlation between first-task and second-task grades fell from 0.41 in the control group to 0.14 in the treatment group, driven by larger gains for participants who scored lower initially.[41] Workers exposed to ChatGPT were 2x as likely to report using it in their real job two weeks after the experiment and 1.6x as likely two months after.[41] In a large-scale field study, the staggered introduction of a generative AI conversational assistant to 5,172 customer-support agents increased productivity, measured by issues resolved per hour, by 15% on average.[42] Field experiments in online retail similarly document treatment effects ranging from 0% to 16.3% in sales, with smaller and newer sellers as well as less experienced consumers exhibiting disproportionately larger gains.[43] A comprehensive review of the empirical literature finds task-completion time reductions of 15% to more than 50% across writing, customer support, software development, accounting, law, and translation.[39] In software development specifically, GitHub Copilot users complete coding tasks 55.8% faster in controlled settings, with larger gains among less experienced developers.[39] A separate study of nearly 5,000 developers across three field experiments finds a 26.08% increase in weekly task completion, again driven by higher adoption and disproportionately larger gains for junior developers.[39] In a randomized trial with 300 professional translators, a tenfold increase in training compute reduced completion time by 12.3%, improved quality by 0.18 standard deviations, and raised earnings per minute by 16.1%, with lower-skilled translators experiencing gains roughly four times larger than higher-skilled peers.[39] These productivity gains exhibit a consistent pattern of skill compression, whereby less-experienced and lower-skilled workers benefit disproportionately from AI assistance.[39][41][42] Exposure measures across multiple studies converge toward high-wage jobs, with correlations between AI exposure and wages ranging from 0.27 to 0.54.[44] A separate review finds that automating firms show 2% to 10% higher employment and positive elasticities between 0.2% and 2%, suggesting complementarity at the firm level even where aggregate displacement is detectable.[45] The analysis finds that a 1% increase in freelancers' earnings is associated with an additional 0.5% reduction in the number of jobs and a 1.7% reduction in monthly income for text-based services.[38]

content.content:
TASK 72 PROMPT:
Please write a literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market. Focus on how AI, as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, is causing significant disruptions and affecting various industries. Ensure the review only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles.

DIMENSION WEIGHTS: {"readability": 0.14, "insight": 0.32, "comprehensiveness": 0.29, "instruction_following": 0.25}

=== COMPREHENSIVENESS (weight 0.29) ===
- [w=0.10] Grounding in AI and the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Assesses whether the review adequately defines AI within the context of the 4IR and explains its fundamental role as a driver of labor market restructuring. This contextual framing is crucial for understanding the scope and nature of the impacts discussed.
- [w=0.25] Breadth of Labor Market Restructuring Dimensions Covered :: Evaluates the extent to which the review addresses the multifaceted nature of AI's impact on the labor market, including job creation, job displacement, job transformation, changes in skill demands, wage effects, and productivity changes.
- [w=0.25] Scope of Industry-Specific Analysis :: Checks if the review examines the restructuring impact of AI across a diverse range of relevant industries, highlighting both common trends and sector-specific nuances. The task explicitly requires focusing on 'various industries.'
- [w=0.15] Exploration of AI's Disruptive Character and Scale :: Assesses whether the review sufficiently explores the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI, including the magnitude, speed, and transformative potential of these changes on labor market structures and dynamics.
- [w=0.15] Depth and Representativeness of Literature Synthesized :: Evaluates if the review synthesizes a broad and representative selection of current, high-quality academic literature (from the specified journal articles), covering the main research themes, findings, and ongoing debates regarding AI's impact on the labor market. This ensures the review's content is built upon a solid and comprehensive foundation of existing knowledge.
- [w=0.10] Balanced Discussion of AI's Labor Market Impacts :: Assesses whether the review presents a nuanced perspective by discussing both the challenges (e.g., displacement, skill gaps, inequality) and opportunities (e.g., new job creation, productivity gains, enhanced work quality) arising from AI's influence on the labor market.

=== INSIGHT (weight 0.32) ===
- [w=0.25] Analytical Depth in Characterizing AI-Driven Labor Market Restructuring Mechanisms :: Assesses if the review moves beyond superficial descriptions of labor market changes to deeply analyze the underlying *mechanisms* (e.g., task automation, skill augmentation, job creation/destruction dynamics, organizational adaptation) through which AI specifically reshapes job roles, skill requirements, and overall labor market structures.
- [w=0.25] Critical Synthesis and Nuanced Evaluation of AI's Disruptive Impacts Across Industries :: Evaluates the review's ability to critically synthesize diverse findings from high-quality journal articles to present a nuanced understanding of AI's 'significant disruptions.' This includes identifying patterns of impact, variations across different industries, and areas of consensus, debate, or uncertainty in the literature, rather than merely cataloging effects.
- [w=0.15] Insightful Integration of AI's Role within the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Measures how effectively the review articulates and leverages the 4IR framework to provide a deeper, more contextualized understanding of the *nature, scale, and interconnectedness* of AI's transformative power on the labor market, rather than treating AI's impact in isolation or merely mentioning the 4IR superficially.
- [w=0.20] Identification and Articulation of Emergent Themes, Theoretical Linkages, or Novel Perspectives :: Assesses whether the review transcends simple summarization to identify and clearly articulate overarching emergent themes, significant theoretical linkages between concepts, or potentially novel perspectives derived from the synthesis of the reviewed literature on AI's labor market impact.
- [w=0.15] Value and Foresight in Delineating Implications and Future Research Agendas :: Evaluates the review's capacity to distill strategically valuable implications (e.g., for policy, education, workforce adaptation strategies) and to clearly delineate critical research gaps or promising future research agendas based on the comprehensive synthesis of the current state of knowledge.

=== INSTRUCTION_FOLLOWING (weight 0.25) ===
- [w=0.10] Adherence to 'Literature Review' Format and Purpose :: Assesses if the article is structured and executed as a literature review, meaning it synthesizes and discusses existing published research on the topic, rather than presenting new empirical findings or being solely an opinion piece.
- [w=0.20] Consistent Focus on 'Restructuring Impact of AI on the Labor Market' :: Evaluates whether the article's content remains consistently and primarily focused on AI's restructuring effects on the labor market, as per the core task instruction, without significant digressions into unrelated AI applications or general economic theory not tied to labor impacts.
- [w=0.15] Integration of 'AI as a Key Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' Theme :: Checks if the review explicitly incorporates and discusses AI within the specific context of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, as mandated by the task's focus requirements.
- [w=0.15] Explicit Addressal of AI-Driven 'Significant Disruptions' in the Labor Market :: Assesses whether the review specifically addresses and elaborates on the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI in the labor market, fulfilling another key focus point of the task.
- [w=0.15] Coverage of AI's Impact on 'Various Industries' :: Evaluates if the review discusses the impact of AI on the labor market across a spectrum of 'various industries,' rather than limiting its scope to a single industry or providing only a generic overview, as per the instruction.
- [w=0.15] Exclusive Citation of 'High-Quality Journal Articles' :: Verifies that all cited sources are academic, peer-reviewed journal articles (or meet a clear standard of 'high-quality' if defined contextually, e.g., leading journals), and that other forms of literature (e.g., books, conference proceedings, news articles, blogs, non-peer-reviewed reports) are not cited, adhering to the 'only cites high-quality... journal articles' constraint.
- [w=0.10] Exclusive Citation of 'English-Language' Journal Articles :: Ensures that all journal articles cited in the review are originally published in English or are established English translations, strictly adhering to the language constraint for sources.

=== READABILITY (weight 0.14) ===
- [w=0.20] L1: Language Clarity, Precision, and Academic Tone :: Evaluates the accuracy and fluency of sentence construction, grammar, spelling, and punctuation. Assesses the precise, consistent, and appropriate use of technical terminology related to AI, labor markets, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, and specific industries. Ensures an objective, formal academic tone suitable for a literature review. For example, terms like 'skill-biased technical change' or specific AI techniques should be used correctly and, if necessary, briefly contextualized for a broader academic audience.
- [w=0.20] S1: Overall Structure and Logical Organization :: Assesses the coherence and logic of the review's macro-structure. This includes a clear introduction defining the review's scope, purpose, and organizational map; logically sequenced thematic sections with informative headings that reflect the facets of AI's impact on labor (e.g., by industry, skill level, type of disruption); and a conclusion that effectively synthesizes the main themes from the literature and perhaps points to gaps or future research directions.
- [w=0.15] S2: Paragraph Cohesion and Transitions :: Evaluates if each paragraph focuses on a single, clear idea or theme derived from the literature, with sentences flowing logically within the paragraph. Assesses the effectiveness of transitional phrases, sentences, and logical connectors in linking ideas smoothly between paragraphs and across different sections of the review, ensuring a coherent narrative thread.
- [w=0.15] P1: Clarity and Synthesis in Presenting Sourced Information :: Assesses how clearly, concisely, and accurately key arguments, findings, theories, and methodologies from the cited journal articles are presented. Evaluates the effectiveness of synthesizing information from multiple sources to build a coherent understanding of topics (e.g., AI's effect on job displacement vs. creation, or changes in skill demands across industries), rather than just serially summarizing individual papers. Checks for appropriate information density and avoidance of redundancy.
- [w=0.10] D1: Clarity of Data/Evidence Referenced or Summarized :: Evaluates the clarity with which quantitative or qualitative data, empirical findings, or specific evidence from cited studies (e.g., statistics on automation rates, survey results on worker sentiment) are described, interpreted, or summarized within the review. If the review itself uses tables or figures to compile or synthesize information from sources (e.g., a summary table of key studies and their findings), assesses their clarity, labeling, and effectiveness in aiding comprehension.
- [w=0.10] F1: Formatting, Layout, and Visual Consistency :: Evaluates the use of consistent and professional formatting (e.g., font choice and size, spacing, margins, heading styles) that enhances readability. Assesses the overall visual layout for ease of navigation and reduced reader fatigue, including clear paragraph breaks. Also considers the visual consistency and unobtrusive integration of in-text citations and the clarity of the bibliography's formatting, which contributes to the academic presentation.
- [w=0.10] A1: Audience Adaptation and Explanation of Terms :: Assesses if the language, level of detail, and style of argumentation are appropriate for an academic audience interested in AI's impact on the labor market. Evaluates whether potentially ambiguous or highly specialized terms are sufficiently contextualized or briefly explained to ensure understanding by readers who may not be experts in every specific sub-domain discussed (e.g., specific AI algorithms or niche economic theories).

cd /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose; python3 - <<'EOF'
import re,collections
t=open('report.md').read()
body=t.split('## References')[0] if '## References' in t else t
sents=re.split(r'(?<=[.\]])\s+(?=[A-Z])', body)
def norm(s):
    s=re.sub(r'\[\d+\]','',s); s=re.sub(r'\W+',' ',s).lower().strip(); return s
c=collections.Counter(norm(s) for s in sents if len(norm(s))>60)
dupes=[(k,v) for k,v in c.items() if v>1]
print("exact-normalized dupes:",len(dupes))
for k,v in dupes[:10]: print(v,k[:120])
print("words body:",len(body.split()))
# near dupes
keys=[k for k in c if len(k)>60]
import itertools
def jac(a,b):
    A=set(a.split());B=set(b.split());return len(A&B)/len(A|B)
near=[(a,b) for a,b in itertools.combinations(keys,2) if jac(a,b)>0.65]
print("near dupes >0.65:",len(near))
for a,b in near[:8]: print("---\n",a[:110],"\n",b[:110])
EOF

content.content:
, and democratic processes.

In conclusion, the literature confirms that AI is a powerful force reshaping labor markets, presenting both significant opportunities for productivity and innovation, and substantial challenges related to displacement, inequality, and the need for workforce adaptation. While theoretical frameworks provide valuable insights, empirical evidence remains complex and often context-dependent. Navigating this transformation successfully will require continued research to fill knowledge gaps, coupled with thoughtful, adaptive, and holistic policy responses aimed at harnessing AI's benefits while mitigating its risks and ensuring a prosperous and equitable future of work.
=== headings:
1:# **The Restructuring Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Labor Market: A Literature Review**
3:## **1\. Introduction: AI, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, and Labor Market Restructuring**
5:### **1.1 Defining the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) and AI's Role**
13:### **1.2 AI as a Catalyst for Labor Market Transformation**
21:## **2\. Theoretical Lenses: Understanding AI's Mechanisms of Impact**
25:### **2.1 The Task-Based Framework: Displacement and Reinstatement Dynamics**
40:### **2.2 Skill-Biased, Task-Biased, and Routine-Biased Technical Change (RBTC)**

content.text:
I dug into the actual files. The lever's factual base is largely wrong, and the measurement math doesn't clear the bar.

**Evidence checks against `/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md`:**

1. **"239 Scopus citations" does not exist in the report.** Grep for `Scopus` → zero hits. Fabricated example.
2. **"Toptal ad content [55]" is not in the body.** `Toptal` appears exactly once, at line 104 — inside the References list. A deletion pass over the composed *body* removes nothing.
3. **The "verbatim twice" duplicate is real but tiny.** Normalized-exact dupes across the body: **0**. Fuzzy (Jaccard > 0.65) near-dupes: **2 pairs total** — the ChatGPT "2x/2 times as likely" pair and one "evidence is inconclusive" pair. That is ~100 words, not 1,500–2,000.
4. Actual flagged padding (SMJ/ALMATO on line 15, "525 papers" on line 43) is a few sentences. Realistic recoverable total: **200–400 words, not 1,500–2,000.**

**The enabler premise is inverted.** Body word counts: our report **7,742 words of body** + ~1,450 words of bibliography = 9,194. The reference `RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md` is **9,029 words, all body — it has zero citation markers and no bibliography at all.** So our *substantive prose is already ~1,300 words SHORTER than the reference's*. There is no length ceiling to fund from; levers 3–4 can simply add synthesis and move *toward* reference parity. "Without it, levers 3-4 blow past 9k words" is a self-imposed constraint against a number that is 16% bibliography.

**Measurement math.** The lever mislabels its own weights: readability dimension is **0.14**, not 0.25. Effective weights: IF "Consistent Focus" = 0.25 × 0.20 = **0.050**; readability P1 = 0.14 × 0.15 = **0.021**. Total **0.071** of the weighted score. With Overall = T/(T+R) = 0.4313, dOverall = Overall·(1−Overall)·Δ(lnT) = 0.245 · Δrel. Netting **+0.005 requires a 2.0% relative gain in the whole weighted score**, concentrated in criteria worth 7.1% → a **~29% relative jump on BOTH criteria** (≈ +1.4 points on a 5-point scale) from deleting 3–5 sentences out of ~300. No judge moves "significant digressions into unrelated AI applications" by a full grade band because four metadata clauses vanished from a 7,700-word review that is otherwise on-topic. And the reference isn't clean-by-comparison on synthesis either — it just has no citations to hang metadata on, so P1 "redundancy" is not where we're losing to it.

**Downside is asymmetric.** Deleting cited facts strictly subtracts from comprehensiveness (0.29 weight; Breadth w=0.25 and Depth/Representativeness w=0.15 = 0.116 effective, 1.6x the two targets combined). Trading 0.116-weight breadth signal for 0.071-weight tidiness is a losing exchange even if the deletion pass works perfectly.

**What actually survives:** the search surfaced a real, judge-visible defect the lever walked past. Source tiers in our bibliography: 17×T1, 12×T2, 17×T3, 28×T4, **2×T5, 6×T6, 1×T7** — including a Toptal freelancer-hiring marketing page cited as `[55]`. That hits IF "Exclusive Citation of 'High-Quality Journal Articles'" (w=0.15 → **0.0375 effective, ~2x readability P1**), a criterion a judge can verify by scanning the reference list in seconds and will penalize hard. Pruning/replacing the 9 non-journal sources is the higher-value move, and it is *citation surgery, not prose deletion*. Also worth 5 minutes: fix the 2 genuine near-dupe sentences. Neither justifies a "deletion pass to free 1,500–2,000 words."

input.lever: Padding cut to fund the synthesis budget

"Toptal [55]" appears only at line 104, inside the References list, not the body -- a body deletion pass removes nothing. Normalized-exact duplicate sentences in the body: 0. Fuzzy near-dupes (Jaccard>0.65): exactly 2 pairs (~100 words) -- the ChatGPT "2x/2 times as likely" pair (real) and one "evidence is inconclusive" pair. Realistic recoverable padding is 200-400 words, not the claimed 1,500-2,000.

ENABLER PREMISE IS INVERTED. Our body = 7,742 words + ~1,450 words of bibliography (105 entries) = 9,194. The reference RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md is 9,029 words of PURE BODY -- zero citation markers, no bibliography. Our substantive prose is already ~1,300 words SHORTER than the reference's. There is no length ceiling to fund from; levers 3-4 can just add synthesis and move toward reference-body parity. "Blows past 9k words" is a self-imposed constraint on a number that is 16% bibliography.

With Overall = T/(T+R) = 0.4313, dOverall = Overall*(1-Overall)*d(lnT) = 0.245 * relative gain, so +0.005 needs a 2.0% relative gain in the TOTAL weighted score -- i.e. a ~29% relative jump (~+1.4 on a 5-pt scale) on BOTH targeted criteria, from deleting 3-5 sentences out of ~300. A judge does not move "significant digressions into unrelated AI applications" by a full grade band for four metadata clauses in a 7,700-word review that is otherwise on-topic.

DOWNSIDE IS ASYMMETRIC. Deleting cited facts subtracts from comprehensiveness (0.29 dim; Breadth 0.25 + Depth/Representativeness 0.15 = 0.116 effective, 1.6x the two targets combined). Trading 0.116-weight breadth for 0.071-weight tidiness loses even if the deletion executes perfectly. Expected value ~0 to slightly negative.
input.salvage: Two real things survive, neither of which is a prose-deletion pass. (1) SOURCE-TIER PRUNING, not padding cutting: the bibliography contains 2xT5, 6xT6, 1xT7 sources including a Toptal freelancer-hiring MARKETING PAGE cited as [55]. This hits IF "Exclusive Citation of High-Quality Journal Articles" (w=0.15 -> 0.0375 effective, ~2x readability P1's 0.021), a criterion a judge verifies by scanning the reference list in seconds and penalizes hard. Replace/remove the 9 non-journal citations. This is citation surgery, and it is the higher-value move. (2) Fix the 2 genuine near-duplicate sentences (the "2x as likely"/"2 times as likely" ChatGPT pair and the "evidence is inconclusive" pair) -- ~free, worth ~0 points, do it in passing. (3) Corollary for the other levers: do NOT budget synthesis words by cutting; our body is 1,300 words below the reference body, so add synthesis outright.
